-128 J Street o Suite 620 o Sacramento. CA g5gl4-232g
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
February 5, 2008
Sandra J. Levin
Colantuono & Levin, pC
555 West 5fi Street, 3l't Floor
Los Angeles, California 90013
RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No.I-08-179
Dear Ms. Levin:
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Sierra Madre Mayor pro Tem
Maryann MacGillivray regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the political Reform Act
(the "Act") I and is baied on the-facts presented; the Fairbolitical practices Commission (the
"Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders advice. (ln re ogtesby (1g75) I
FPPC ops' 7l') Additionally, we base our advice solely on rhe provisions of the Act and do not
address the applicability, if any, of other conflicrof-interest lawi, such as .orn*on law conflict of
interest or Govemment code Section 1090. Because you provided various tyf., or decisions that
may be presented, without any details, and have not iientiiied any specific gf'r"-*.rtal decision
that is pending before the city council, we are providing informal'asriri*.Jt -
Does Sierra Madre Mayor Pro Tem Maryann MacGillivray have a conflict of interest
participating in govemmental decisions involving a housing project when part of the project is
located within 500 feet of her home?
CONCLUSION
Yes' Mayor Pro Tem Maryann MacGillivray has a potential conflict-of-interest and may
not participate in any decision that involves property that is located within 500 feet of her
I rhe Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code sections g 1000 through gl0l4. AII statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair political practices
Commission are contained in sections l8l l0 through 18997 of ritle 2 of tire California Coae of Regulations. All
regulatory ref'erences are to Title 2, Division 6 of thi Calitornia coae oi'Regulations. unless otherwise indicated.
r Inftrrmal assistance does not provide the requesmr with the immunity provided by an opinion or ftrrmal
written advice. (Section g3l l4: Regulaiion 1g329(c)(i), .upy .n.tur.Jl.
QUESTION


File No. I-08-179
Page No.2
property unless the decision will not have any financial effect on her property or the financial
effect is not distinguishable from its effect on the public generally.
FACTS
Mayor Pro Tem MacGillivray and her husband own a single-family home located in Sierra
Madre. The home is a single story residence on a lot containing approximately 14,110 square feet
of land. The property is located within an R-l single family resiaintiat zoninj disirict. I 
- --
The One Carter Project (the "Project"), located at the northeast quadrant of Carter Avenue
and Elm Avenue in Sierra Madre, consists of 29 numbered single-family residential lots and four
lettered lots. The Project encompasses 32.044 acres. The MacGillivray residence is located
within 300 feet of Lot C, a designated open space area, and within 500ieet of Lots three, four,
five, six, and seven, designated single-family residential sites.
You anticipate that members of the city council will be presented with various decisions
regarding the Project. These decisions may include the following: (l) consideration of
applications for the development of homes; (2) considerations of apptications to modify the
existing Tract Map, to increase or decrease development density, foi conditional use permits,
zone variances, and zoning ordinances; (3) enforcement of exisling conditions of appioval;
(4) decisions concerning the Deferred Improvement Agreement; (5) bond and relatid rights of the
city with respect to the Project; and (6) the defense and/or settlement of pending litigati[n
regarding the rights of the city in relation to the project.
You have also submitted a copy of an appraisal report performed by a Califomia licens"ed
certified commercial appraiser located in Long Beach, *hi"h we have revilwed. This report
concludes that, subject-to certain exceptions, "the above-referenced decisions pertaining io the
One Carter Project will not have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on the market
value, . . . of [Mayor Pro Tem MacGillivray's] personal residence -. . ..,,3
ANALYSIS
The Act's conflicrof-interest provisions ensure that public officials will perform their
duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the
financial interest of persons who have supported them. (section 81001(b).) specifically, section
87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his
or her official position to influence a govemmental deciiion in which the official has a finaicial
interest.
I The exceptions apply to "to any decision regarding ( I ) an amendment to the existing conditions of
approval [that] would permit the development of Lot C, which is within the viewshed of [the] iesidence, and/or (2) an
increase in the development density or height limit to all or portions of the project, ueyono thtse currently permitted
in the R- I zone."


File No. t-08-179
Page No. 3
A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision, within the meaning
of the Act, when it is "reasonably foreseeable" that the governmental decision will have a
material financial effect on one or more of the public official's economic interests. (Section
87103; Regulation 18700(a).) In order to determine whether a public official has a disqualifying
conflict of interest in a given govemmental decision, the Commission has adopted un "igt t-.iei
analytical framework. (Regulation 18700(bXl)-(8).) The general rule, however, is that a "onfii"t
of interest exists whenever a public official makes a governmental decision that has a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of his or her financial interests. (Section
87103.)
Srnp ONn: Is M,lyon Pno Tsrvr M^lcGu,r,rvRAy A puBLIC Orrrct.lr,?
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to "public officials." (Section g7100.)
A "public official" is "every member, officer, employee, or consultant of a state or local
government agency." (Section 82048, Regulation 18701.) .Members of the Sierra Madre City
Council are public officials within the meaning of the Act.a
srnp Two: wn r, M,q,von pno TBur M,q,cG[LrvRAy BE MAKTNG, pARTrcrpATrNG rN
M.lrrNc, oR INFLUENCTNG A GovERNMENTAL Dncrsron?
A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting within the
purview of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or commits his or her agency
to any course of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or her ug"i"y. 
-
(Regulation 18702.1.)
A public official "participates in making" a governmental decision when he or she,
without substantive_r9.uii*, negotiates, advises, or makes recommendations regarding a decision.
(Regulation 18702.2.)" A public official is "influencing a governmental decision" if he or she
contacts, or appears before, or otherwise attempts to influence any member, officer, employee, or
consultant of the city regarding the decisions. (Regulation lg7oz.3.)
Mayor Pro Tem MacGillivray will be called upon to consider and vote on certain
governmental decisions regarding the Project. Therefore, she will be making, participating in
making, or otherwise using her official position to influence a govemmental dicision.
a If a public official's office is listed in Section 872W (*872o}filers" include city council members) and he
or she has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a- public meeting, then he or she must: ( I ) immediately prior to
the discussion of the item' verbally identify each type of economic inteiest involved in the decision as well as details
of the economic interest' as discussed in Regulation 18702.5(bX I )(B), on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse
himself or herself; and (3) leave the room for the duration of the discussion and/or vote on the item. For closed
sessions, consent calendars, absences and speaking as a member ofthe public regarding personal interests, special
rules found in Regulation 18702.5, subdivisions (c) and (d) apply. (Seciion g7105.)
' Making or participating in a governmental decision does not include appearances by a public official as a
membel of the general public before an agency in the course of its prescribed gorirnmental functlon to represent
himself or herself on matters related solely to the official's wholly owned real-property or business entity. (See
Regulation 18702.4.)


File No. I-08-179
Page No.4
SrBp THRB: WH.lr ARE MAYoR PRo Tru MecG[LIvRAy's EcoNoMIC INTEREsTs-TrIE
Possmr,B Souncns oF CoNFLrcr oF lxrnRrsr ron Hrn?
A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on
the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her immediate family, or on any one of
five enumerated economic interests. (Section 87103; Regulations 18703-lgiOf .S.) fni
applicable economic interests include:
o . An economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or indirect
investment of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(a); Regulation lg703.l(a)); or in
which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any
position of managemenr (Section 87103(d); Regulation lg703.l(b));
o An economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect
interest of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(b); Regulation lg7O3.Z);
o An economic interest in any source of income, including promised income that
aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (Section
87 103(c); Regulation 1 8703.3);
o An economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts aggregate to
$390 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (Section STf Ofte-inegulation
18703.4):
o A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal expenses, income,
assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate family-. This is also
knows as the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103, Regulation
18703.5.)
The only potential economic interest for which you have provided facts is Mayor pro Tem
MacGillivray's interest in the real property used as her residence.6 Because you havanot
provided any information (nor asked for an analysis of) any other potential economic interest, we
will limit our analysis to Mayor Pro Tem MacGillivray's real p.opirty economic interest in her
home.
Srrr Foun: Is M.lvoR PRo Tnu MecG[LIvRAy's EcoNoMrc INTEREsT DTRECTLy oR Nor
Drnrcrly Ixvolvnn rN THE GovBnNuENTAL Drcrslox?
Real property in which a public official has an economic interest is considered directly
involved if the property is located within 500 feet of the boundaries of the property that is the
subject of the governmental decision. (Regulation 1g704.2(aXl).)
6 we assume that her interest in that property is valued at $2,(D0 or more


File No. I-08-179
Page No. 5
Under the facts you have presented, the real property that is the subject of any of the
anticipated governmental decisions could be either the entire Project, indivldual lots that are
within 500 feet of the MacGillivray property, or individual lots that are not within 500 feet of the
property.
Accordingly, the determination of whether or not Mayor Pro Tem MacGillivray's
economic interest in her real property is directly involved or not directly involved is dipendent on
which property is the subject of any particular decision.
srnpFryr: wHer Anr rnn Apptrcnnr,E MATERTALTTy STANDARDS?
Regulation 18705.2(b)(1) provides that the financial effect of a governmental decision on
real property that is indirectly involvedT in the governmental decision is-presumed not to be
material.
"This presumption may be rebutted by proof that there are specific circumstances
regarding the governmental decision, its financial effect, and the nature of the real
property in which the public official has an economic interest, [that] make it
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financtl effect on the
real property in which the public official has an interest. Examples of specific
circumstances that will be considered include, but are not limited to, ciriumstances
where the decision affects:
"(A) The development potential or income producing potential of the real
property in which the official has an economic interest;
"(B) The use of real property in which the official has an economic
interest;
"(C) The character of the neighborhood including, but not limited to,
substantial effects on: traffic, view, privacy, intensity of use, noise levels, air
emissions, or similar traits of the neighborhood."
From the facts presented, it does not appear that decisions involving properties located
more than 500 feet from the MacGillivray property would present circumstances to rebut the
presumption that there will be no material financial effect on the MacGillivray property.
However, it is up to the public official to analyze and consider these factors ui tlt " 
time the
decision is presented.
For purposes of applying the materiality .standards, property that is deemed to be not directly involved
under Step Four is considered "indirectly involved."


File No. I-08-179
Page No. 6
Regulation 18705.2(a)(1) provides the materiality standard for directly involved real
property as follows:
"The financial effect on the real property is presumed to be material. This
presumption may be rebutted by proof that it is not reasonably foreseeable that
the govemmental decision will have anyfinancial effecton th; real property.,,
(Emphasis added.)
Under this rule, the financial effect of the decision is material even if it has only a one
penny effect. This is known as the ,.one penny ruIe."
The Appraisal Report
You have included with your request for advice a copy of an appraisal report prepared by
a california licensed certified commercial appraiser. subject to certain exceptions, the report
concludes that none of the city counsel decisi,ons enumerited above will have a reasonably
foreseeable financial effect on the market value of Mayor pro Tem MacGillivray,s personal
residence' while' as mentioned, the Commission does not act as a finder of fact when rendering
advice (see In re oglesby, supra), we nevertheless reserve the right to r.ui"* professional
opinions submitted to us as part of advice requests to determin" If th"y conectiy apply
commission regulations and that the conclusion has some reasonable-basis for support. As set
forth below, we have reviewed the appraisal and conclude that it does not contain sufficient
information to overcome the presumption stated in Regulation 1g705.2(a)(l).
The question for which this appraisal report was solicited is: what financial effect, if any,
will there be on the official's property as a result of certain govemmental decisions that affect
property nearby. Part of the theory upon which conflict of interest determinations are based is
th-at, for real property, decisions made affecting one property have a tendencfio affect the values
9^f lht su-rrounding properties. The commission has developed a brighrline *t" in this regard -
if the official's property is within 500 feet of the property tliat is theiubject of the decision, a
material financial effect (one penny or more) is piesumei. lr tn. p.op".ty is more than 500 feet
Io- tt property that is the subject of the decision, the presumption is that there is no material
rmanclal ettect.
- 
Accordingly, in order to rebut the presumption of a material financial effect, which has
been established here, the appraisal must support a conclusion that the particular governmental
decision under consideration will have no financial effect whatsoever on the MacGillivray
residence' while the appraisal provides us with a conclusion to that effect, it provides little
support for its findings as discussed below. 
- -' -- r-
First, the report provides no discussion as to why the past governmental decisions are
relevant to the determination of future ones that may afiect the Ma'cGillivray residence. Even if
we accept as fact that none of the past decisions financially affected the Maccillivray residence,
that conclusion does not necessarily inform us as to whether any future decisions will.


File No. I-08-179
Page No. 7
oftentimes preliminary decisions are inconsequential when compared with the later, more
substantive decisions.
Next, even if we assume there is a significant relationship between past and future
decisions from which we can conclude that the effects of future decisions will be no different
from the effects of past decisions, we question whether the graph that accompanies the report
necessarily supports a finding that the past governmental decisions had no financial effect on the
MacGillivray property.
There is no explanation given for how the graph was formulated. Various sales are
indicated as dots, but there is no indication as to whictr sales were of properties in the immediate
area of the Project. Moreover, while the graph indicates the overall sales-trends for real propertt
in Sierra Madre during the times the city wis making decisions on the project, the scale used is
far to large to reflect whether or not any of the decisions did or did not havl a one-penny effect on
any of those sales
Finally, the report selects certain "comparables"8 to create the "significant segment.,,e
However, none of the parameters selected for use in identifying these coriparables are important
to the analysis of whether a particular governmental decision affe"ts the value of property located
within 500 feet of property that is the subject of the decision. In this case, we are not trying to
establish the value of the home. For example, the comparables selected in the appraisal report are
based upon size (among other things) and not upon location. If the goal is to measure the
financial effect of a decision concerning one property on property nearby, it seems the first
criterion for selecting comparables should ueltre properties niarby and the other factors
considered are not relevant.
AccordinglY, we do not believe the appraisal report supports a conclusion that city
$9cis1ons regarding the Project will have no iinanciat irect uiutt o, Mayor pro-Tem
MacGillivray's property. Therefore, we cannot accept it for purpor", of d.t"r-ining that the
proposed decisions will have no financial effect on the VtacC-illivray propertt. 
-
.. 
i'l a typical appraisal report used to determine a property's value under the ..sales comparison approach,,,
"comparables" are those sale properties that are the most slmiiar io the subject property with, tbr example, a model
match two houses down the street on a lot the same size that closed erc.o* tasi*eet being a areum match. Lacking
that, homes that are the most similar to the subject property are used.
e As used in the appraisal report, the term "significant segment" means "those sales properties [that; are
reasonably comparable to the subject property." This should not*be confused with the use of the term significant
segment under the Commission's public generally exception. In the appraisal report, tr,e signincant segment is
composed of the "comparable" properties selected by the appraiser and'the "comparable" pffirties selected by the
appraiser comprise the significant segment. In ordei to avoid any confusion wittr the ,r. of th. tr.m under the public
generally exception, we will use the term comparables instead of significant segment as they are, essentially, the
same thing.


File No. I-08-179
Page No. 8
Srnp Sx: Rr,lsoNenlE FoRESEEABTLTTy
whether the. financial consequences of a decision are reasonably foreseeable at the time a
govemmental decision is made depends on the facts of each particular case.
A financial effect need not be a certainty to be considered reasonably foreseeable; a substantial
likelihood that it will occur suffices to meet the standard. (Regulation TSZOO; In re Thorner
(I975) I FPPC ops. 198.) on the other hand, if an effect is only a mere porribility, it is not
reasonably foreseeable . (lbid.) Please note that the Commission does noi act as a finder of fact
when providing advice. (In re Oglesby, supra,l FppC Ops. 71.)
Srrp SBvUN: Punr,rc GpNrnar,r,y
You have not presented any facts indicating that the "public generally" exception may
apply. Given the substantial likelihood that at leasi some of tir" gouJ-mental decisions ,"gurdirg
the one Carter Project will have a reasonably foreseeable materiil financial effect on the
MacGillivray property you may wish to have us consider this exception if you write in for future
assistance.
Srnp ErcHr: Lrcu,r,y REeuTRED pARTrcrpATroN
You have not presented any facts to indicate that the "legally required participation,,
exception is applicable.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
Counsel
\
By: William
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

Regulation 18705.2(b)(1) provides that the financial effect of a governmental decision on
real property that is indirectly involvedT in the governmental decision is-presumed not to be
material.
"This presumption may be rebutted by proof that there are specific circumstances
regarding the governmental decision, its financial effect, and the nature of the real
property in which the public official has an economic interest, [that] make it
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financtl effect on the
real property in which the public official has an interest. Examples of specific
circumstances that will be considered include, but are not limited to, ciriumstances
where the decision affects:
"(A) The development potential or income producing potential of the real
property in which the official has an economic interest;
"(B) The use of real property in which the official has an economic
interest;
"(C) The character of the neighborhood including, but not limited to,
substantial effects on: traffic, view, privacy, intensity of use, noise levels, air
emissions, or similar traits of the neighborhood."
From the facts presented, it does not appear that decisions involving properties located
more than 500 feet from the MacGillivray property would present circumstances to rebut the
presumption that there will be no material financial effect on the MacGillivray property.
However, it is up to the public official to analyze and consider these factors ui tlt " 
time the
decision is presented.
For purposes of applying the materiality .standards, property that is deemed to be not directly involved
under Step Four is considered "indirectly involved."
Regulation 18705.2(a)(1) provides the materiality standard for directly involved real
property as follows:
"The financial effect on the real property is presumed to be material. This
presumption may be rebutted by proof that it is not reasonably foreseeable that
the govemmental decision will have anyfinancial effecton th; real property.,,
(Emphasis added.)
Under this rule, the financial effect of the decision is material even if it has only a one
penny effect. This is known as the ,.one penny ruIe."
The Appraisal Report
You have included with your request for advice a copy of an appraisal report prepared by
a california licensed certified commercial appraiser. subject to certain exceptions, the report
concludes that none of the city counsel decisi,ons enumerited above will have a reasonably
foreseeable financial effect on the market value of Mayor pro Tem MacGillivray,s personal
residence' while' as mentioned, the Commission does not act as a finder of fact when rendering
advice (see In re oglesby, supra), we nevertheless reserve the right to r.ui"* professional
opinions submitted to us as part of advice requests to determin" If th"y conectiy apply
commission regulations and that the conclusion has some reasonable-basis for support. As set
forth below, we have reviewed the appraisal and conclude that it does not contain sufficient
information to overcome the presumption stated in Regulation 1g705.2(a)(l).
The question for which this appraisal report was solicited is: what financial effect, if any,
will there be on the official's property as a result of certain govemmental decisions that affect
property nearby. Part of the theory upon which conflict of interest determinations are based is
th-at, for real property, decisions made affecting one property have a tendencfio affect the values
9^f lht su-rrounding properties. The commission has developed a brighrline *t" in this regard -
if the official's property is within 500 feet of the property tliat is theiubject of the decision, a
material financial effect (one penny or more) is piesumei. lr tn. p.op".ty is more than 500 feet
Io- tt property that is the subject of the decision, the presumption is that there is no material
rmanclal ettect.
- 
Accordingly, in order to rebut the presumption of a material financial effect, which has
been established here, the appraisal must support a conclusion that the particular governmental
decision under consideration will have no financial effect whatsoever on the MacGillivray
residence' while the appraisal provides us with a conclusion to that effect, it provides little
support for its findings as discussed below. 
- -' -- r-
First, the report provides no discussion as to why the past governmental decisions are
relevant to the determination of future ones that may afiect the Ma'cGillivray residence. Even if
we accept as fact that none of the past decisions financially affected the Maccillivray residence,
that conclusion does not necessarily inform us as to whether any future decisions will.
oftentimes preliminary decisions are inconsequential when compared with the later, more
substantive decisions.
Next, even if we assume there is a significant relationship between past and future
decisions from which we can conclude that the effects of future decisions will be no different
from the effects of past decisions, we question whether the graph that accompanies the report
necessarily supports a finding that the past governmental decisions had no financial effect on the
MacGillivray property.
There is no explanation given for how the graph was formulated. Various sales are
indicated as dots, but there is no indication as to whictr sales were of properties in the immediate
area of the Project. Moreover, while the graph indicates the overall sales-trends for real propertt
in Sierra Madre during the times the city wis making decisions on the project, the scale used is
far to large to reflect whether or not any of the decisions did or did not havl a one-penny effect on
any of those sales
Finally, the report selects certain "comparables"8 to create the "significant segment.,,e
However, none of the parameters selected for use in identifying these coriparables are important
to the analysis of whether a particular governmental decision affe"ts the value of property located
within 500 feet of property that is the subject of the decision. In this case, we are not trying to
establish the value of the home. For example, the comparables selected in the appraisal report are
based upon size (among other things) and not upon location. If the goal is to measure the
financial effect of a decision concerning one property on property nearby, it seems the first
criterion for selecting comparables should ueltre properties niarby and the other factors
considered are not relevant.
AccordinglY, we do not believe the appraisal report supports a conclusion that city
$9cis1ons regarding the Project will have no iinanciat irect uiutt o, Mayor pro-Tem
MacGillivray's property. Therefore, we cannot accept it for purpor", of d.t"r-ining that the
proposed decisions will have no financial effect on the VtacC-illivray propertt. 
-
.. 
i'l a typical appraisal report used to determine a property's value under the ..sales comparison approach,,,
"comparables" are those sale properties that are the most slmiiar io the subject property with, tbr example, a model
match two houses down the street on a lot the same size that closed erc.o* tasi*eet being a areum match. Lacking
that, homes that are the most similar to the subject property are used.
e As used in the appraisal report, the term "significant segment" means "those sales properties [that; are
reasonably comparable to the subject property." This should not*be confused with the use of the term significant
segment under the Commission's public generally exception. In the appraisal report, tr,e signincant segment is
composed of the "comparable" properties selected by the appraiser and'the "comparable" pffirties selected by the
appraiser comprise the significant segment. In ordei to avoid any confusion wittr the ,r. of th. tr.m under the public
generally exception, we will use the term comparables instead of significant segment as they are, essentially, the
same thing.
Srnp Sx: Rr,lsoNenlE FoRESEEABTLTTy
whether the. financial consequences of a decision are reasonably foreseeable at the time a
govemmental decision is made depends on the facts of each particular case.
A financial effect need not be a certainty to be considered reasonably foreseeable; a substantial
likelihood that it will occur suffices to meet the standard. (Regulation TSZOO; In re Thorner
(I975) I FPPC ops. 198.) on the other hand, if an effect is only a mere porribility, it is not
reasonably foreseeable . (lbid.) Please note that the Commission does noi act as a finder of fact
when providing advice. (In re Oglesby, supra,l FppC Ops. 71.)
Srrp SBvUN: Punr,rc GpNrnar,r,y
You have not presented any facts indicating that the "public generally" exception may
apply. Given the substantial likelihood that at leasi some of tir" gouJ-mental decisions ,"gurdirg
the one Carter Project will have a reasonably foreseeable materiil financial effect on the
MacGillivray property you may wish to have us consider this exception if you write in for future
assistance.
Srnp ErcHr: Lrcu,r,y REeuTRED pARTrcrpATroN
You have not presented any facts to indicate that the "legally required participation,,
exception is applicable.

I - -- The One Carter Project (the "Project"), located at the northeast quadrant of Carter Avenue and Elm Avenue in Sierra Madre, consists of 29 numbered single-family residential lots and four lettered lots. The Project encompasses 32.044 acres.
